full_title: Prediction model Risk Of Bias ASsessment Tool — Artificial Intelligence extension
source: Wolff RF, Moons KGM, Riley RD, et al. PROBAST+AI: updated guidance for assessing risk of bias and applicability of AI/ML clinical prediction models. BMJ. 2025 (forthcoming); adapted from PROBAST 2019 (Ann Intern Med 2019;170:51–58).
description: PROBAST+AI is a structured tool for assessing risk of bias (ROB) and applicability of clinical prediction model studies that use AI/ML methods. Reviewers answer 34 signalling questions across 4 domains, each rated: Yes / Probably Yes / No / Probably No / No Information. Domain ROB is judged as: Low / High / Unclear. Overall ROB = Low only if all 4 domains are Low.

answer_options.Y: Yes — clear evidence supporting low risk of bias
answer_options.PY: Probably Yes — likely low risk but not fully documented
answer_options.PN: Probably No — likely some risk of bias present
answer_options.N: No — clear evidence of high risk of bias
answer_options.NI: No Information — insufficient information to judge

domain_rob_rules.low: All signalling questions answered Y or PY
domain_rob_rules.high: At least one question answered N or PN
domain_rob_rules.unclear: At least one question answered NI and no N/PN answers

overall_rob_rule: Overall ROB is Low only when all 4 domains are judged Low. Any single High domain → Overall High.

D1_participants.name: Participant Selection
D1_participants.description: Assesses whether the selection of participants introduces bias. Key concerns: inappropriate inclusion/exclusion criteria, non-representative sampling, case-control design for prognostic models.

signalling_questions.question: Was the source population appropriate for the prediction model's intended use?
signalling_questions.detail: The source population should match the intended clinical population. Convenience samples, highly selected cohorts, or case-control designs may introduce selection bias for prediction model development.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Non-representative source population inflates apparent model performance

signalling_questions.question: Were all potentially eligible participants included in the analysis (or were exclusions appropriate and documented)?
signalling_questions.detail: Arbitrary exclusions or exclusions based on outcome-linked characteristics (e.g., excluding patients who died before the prediction horizon) can distort the training population.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Non-random exclusion can create artificially cleaner data and inflate performance

signalling_questions.question: Were inclusion and exclusion criteria pre-specified before data analysis began?
signalling_questions.detail: Post-hoc modification of eligibility criteria based on observed outcomes constitutes outcome-driven participant selection.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Post-hoc eligibility modifications can constitute undocumented data leakage

signalling_questions.question: Was the study design appropriate for the prediction model's development phase (development vs. external validation)?
signalling_questions.detail: Development studies require sufficient sample size and follow-up. Validation studies should use an independent cohort from a different setting, period, or country.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Using the same data source for both development and 'external' validation is internal validation only

signalling_questions.question: Were participants enrolled consecutively or using a random sample (not selectively)?
signalling_questions.detail: Selective enrollment (e.g., enrolling only patients with complete data, or only certain disease severities) undermines generalizability.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Cherry-picking patients creates optimistic performance that does not replicate

signalling_questions.question: For AI models trained on routine/registry data: was the cohort definition documented with a reproducible phenotyping algorithm?
signalling_questions.detail: Real-world data requires a formal phenotyping definition (e.g., ICD-code-based cohort algorithm) to ensure reproducibility and avoid phenotype-definition leakage.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Undocumented phenotyping introduces definition leakage (outcome derived from same codes used as features)

signalling_questions.question: Was patient-level data partitioning applied (not row-level) to prevent patients appearing in multiple splits?
signalling_questions.detail: Row-level splits that allow the same patient to appear in both train and test sets introduce patient-level leakage and invalidate all performance estimates.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Patient-level overlap between splits inflates all metrics and is a critical data leakage form

signalling_questions.question: For temporal data: was an out-of-time (temporal) split used with test data chronologically after training data?
signalling_questions.detail: For time-stamped medical records, random splitting allows future data to inform historical training, introducing temporal leakage.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Random splits on temporal data inflate performance by allowing future-to-past information flow

D2_predictors.name: Predictors / Features
D2_predictors.description: Assesses whether predictors (features) were defined, measured, and selected in ways that avoid bias. Key AI concerns: feature leakage, post-index features, target encoding leakage, preprocessing leakage.

signalling_questions.question: Were all predictors (features) defined and operationalized before model development?
signalling_questions.detail: Feature definitions should be pre-specified in a feature group specification. Definitions derived after observing the outcome data constitute circular reasoning.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Outcome-informed feature engineering is a subtle form of data leakage

signalling_questions.question: Were all predictors available at the intended time of prediction (no post-index or future-informed features)?
signalling_questions.detail: All features must have been measurable at or before the prediction index time point t_index. Features derived from information after t_index (e.g., downstream tests, subsequent diagnoses) constitute temporal leakage.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Post-index features provide information the model would not have in real clinical use

signalling_questions.question: Were disease-defining variables (phenotype-defining ICD codes, lab tests, or procedures) excluded from the predictor set?
signalling_questions.detail: Using the same variables that define case status as predictors creates perfect or near-perfect leakage (circular prediction).
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Definition-variable leakage is one of the most common critical errors in EHR prediction studies

signalling_questions.question: Were derived features documented with a feature lineage provenance chain?
signalling_questions.detail: Derived features (ratios, aggregations, engineered variables) can inherit leakage from their source variables. A lineage specification documents the transformation chain.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Lineage leakage occurs when derived features trace back to forbidden ancestor variables

signalling_questions.question: Were all preprocessing transformations (scaling, encoding, imputation) fitted exclusively on training data and then applied to validation/test?
signalling_questions.detail: Fitting preprocessing on the full dataset (including validation/test) before splitting causes target statistics and distribution parameters to bleed from test to train, invalidating test performance estimates.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Preprocessing leakage is the most common form of pipeline isolation failure in ML studies

signalling_questions.question: Was feature selection performed within cross-validation folds only (not on the full dataset)?
signalling_questions.detail: Applying feature selection globally before CV allows test-set outcome information to influence which features are selected, inflating apparent performance.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Global feature selection is selection leakage — a well-documented source of optimistic bias in ML pipelines

signalling_questions.question: Was the handling of missing predictor data appropriate and documented?
signalling_questions.detail: Ad hoc deletion or non-documented imputation introduces missing data bias. Multiple imputation or documented deterministic strategies are preferred.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Listwise deletion removes informative missing patterns and biases estimates

signalling_questions.question: Were class imbalance handling techniques (SMOTE, oversampling, undersampling, class weights) applied to training data only?
signalling_questions.detail: Applying resampling before splitting, or to the validation/test sets, artificially inflates minority-class performance and violates split isolation.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Resampling-on-full-dataset is a documented source of gross optimism in imbalanced medical classification

D3_outcome.name: Outcome
D3_outcome.description: Assesses whether outcome measurement is appropriate and unbiased. Key concerns: outcome definition clarity, timing, blinding, and differential verification.

signalling_questions.question: Was the outcome clearly defined with an unambiguous, pre-specified operationalization?
signalling_questions.detail: The outcome should have a formal definition (ICD codes + date logic, lab threshold + time window, etc.) documented before data extraction.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Vague outcome definitions allow post-hoc selection that inflates apparent discrimination

signalling_questions.question: Was the time horizon for prediction clearly specified and consistently applied?
signalling_questions.detail: For prognostic models, the prediction window (e.g., 30-day mortality, 1-year readmission) must be fixed and applied consistently to all participants.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Variable or undefined prediction horizons create outcome misclassification

signalling_questions.question: Was the outcome assessed without knowledge of predictor values (or was blinding not feasible and documented)?
signalling_questions.detail: For manually adjudicated outcomes, knowledge of predictor values during outcome assessment introduces differential measurement bias.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Unblinded outcome assessment inflates perceived discrimination for models using clinician-derived features

signalling_questions.question: Was the same outcome definition applied consistently across all data partitions (train, validation, test) and external validation cohorts?
signalling_questions.detail: Differences in outcome coding across splits (e.g., stricter coding in one hospital, different ICD code versions) cause distribution shift in the outcome label.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Outcome definition inconsistency across partitions creates artificial distribution shift

signalling_questions.question: Was the event rate / prevalence documented per data partition, with sufficient events for the analysis?
signalling_questions.detail: Low event rates (<50 events in validation/test) produce unstable performance estimates with wide confidence intervals that cannot support publication claims.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Insufficient events per partition produce unreliable metrics even if global sample size is adequate

signalling_questions.question: For AI models with EHR outcomes: was the outcome free from coding artifacts that could leak future information (e.g., retrospective chart review codes, encounter-level billing codes)?
signalling_questions.detail: EHR billing codes are sometimes assigned retrospectively or capture diagnoses confirmed after the prediction time point, introducing temporal outcome leakage.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Retrospectively assigned diagnosis codes after t_index constitute outcome-timing leakage

signalling_questions.question: Was censoring (for time-to-event outcomes) or missing outcome data handled appropriately?
signalling_questions.detail: For survival/time-to-event outcomes, informative censoring must be assessed. For binary outcomes, missing outcome classification must be documented.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Informative censoring biases survival estimates; ad hoc outcome imputation creates outcome leakage

D4_analysis.name: Analysis
D4_analysis.description: Assesses whether the analysis is appropriate, complete, and free from bias. Key AI concerns: hyperparameter tuning isolation, model selection rigor, appropriate performance metrics, calibration, DCA, and overfitting assessment.

signalling_questions.question: Was hyperparameter tuning performed only on training/validation data, without any access to test set performance?
signalling_questions.detail: Using test set results to guide hyperparameter selection (even informally) inflates test set performance estimates. All tuning must occur exclusively on training/validation folds.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Test-set peeking during tuning is one of the most impactful sources of bias in published ML studies

signalling_questions.question: Was the model selection strategy documented with a pre-specified primary metric and selection criterion?
signalling_questions.detail: Ad hoc model selection (trying multiple models and reporting the best) without a pre-specified criterion inflates selection bias. The one-SE rule or similar principled criterion should be documented.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Undocumented model selection from a large candidate pool inflates published performance via selection bias

signalling_questions.question: Was a clinically meaningful baseline comparator reported alongside the model?
signalling_questions.detail: Model performance must be compared against a relevant baseline (prevalence model, logistic regression, or existing clinical score) to establish added value.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Reporting performance without baseline comparison obscures whether the model provides any clinical improvement

signalling_questions.question: Was model discrimination reported with bootstrap confidence intervals (not just point estimates)?
signalling_questions.detail: Point estimates without uncertainty quantification are insufficient for publication. Bootstrap-based 95% CI with ≥200 resamples is the minimum standard.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Reporting without CI prevents assessment of estimate precision and clinical significance

signalling_questions.question: Was calibration assessed using appropriate methods (reliability diagram, ECE, calibration slope/intercept, or calibration-in-the-large)?
signalling_questions.detail: Calibration measures whether predicted probabilities match observed event rates. AUC alone is insufficient — a model can have high discrimination but be perfectly miscalibrated.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Uncalibrated models used clinically cause systematic over-treatment or under-treatment

signalling_questions.question: Was decision curve analysis (DCA) or another clinical utility analysis performed?
signalling_questions.detail: DCA quantifies net benefit across threshold probabilities and directly answers whether the model provides clinical value over treat-all or treat-none strategies.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Without DCA or NRI/IDI, it is unclear whether statistical significance translates to clinical utility

signalling_questions.question: Was a statistical test for model significance (e.g., permutation test, likelihood ratio test) performed?
signalling_questions.detail: A permutation test or equivalent establishes that observed performance exceeds chance expectation, ruling out lucky random splits.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Without significance testing, apparent performance may be within chance variation on small datasets

signalling_questions.question: Was overfitting assessed by comparing training/validation performance to test performance?
signalling_questions.detail: Large gaps between training and test performance indicate overfitting. The generalization gap must be documented and be within acceptable bounds.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Unreported overfitting masks models that will not replicate in practice

signalling_questions.question: Was seed stability / seed sensitivity analysis performed to ensure results are not dependent on a single lucky random seed?
signalling_questions.detail: For stochastic algorithms, results can vary substantially across random seeds. Reporting a single seed without seed sensitivity analysis may represent cherry-picking.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Cherry-picked seeds in stochastic ML models inflate reported performance above true expected performance

signalling_questions.question: Was external validation performed on an independent cohort from a different setting, time period, or geographic location?
signalling_questions.detail: Internal validation demonstrates optimism-corrected performance. External validation provides evidence of generalizability to new settings and is required for high-impact publications.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Without external validation, performance claims are limited to the development population

signalling_questions.question: Were all reported performance metrics consistent (e.g., confusion matrix entries sum correctly, sensitivity/specificity/PPV/NPV consistent with each other and the stated threshold)?
signalling_questions.detail: Arithmetic inconsistencies in reported metrics indicate errors in analysis or reporting that undermine trust in all reported results.
signalling_questions.rob_concern: Metric inconsistency is a marker of copy-paste errors or computational bugs in reporting

supplementary_domain_ai.name: AI-Specific Supplementary Assessment
supplementary_domain_ai.description: Additional signalling questions specific to AI/ML models that do not fit cleanly within the four main PROBAST domains. These are recommended for comprehensive reviews of AI prediction models.
supplementary_domain_ai.note: This domain is a MLGG extension to PROBAST+AI to cover aspects unique to the ML lifecycle not fully addressed in the original 4-domain structure.

signalling_questions.question: Was the prediction task definition (binary vs multiclass vs regression) appropriate for the clinical question?
signalling_questions.detail: Binarizing a continuous outcome (e.g., converting HbA1c to diabetic/non-diabetic) discards clinical nuance. Similarly, treating an ordinal outcome as binary may be inappropriate.

signalling_questions.question: Were model artifacts (trained weights, preprocessing pipelines, configuration files) cryptographically signed and preserved for independent verification?
signalling_questions.detail: Reproducibility requires that reviewers can re-run or verify the model. Signed artifacts with hash verification enable independent audit.

signalling_questions.question: Was subgroup fairness analysis performed across protected attributes (age, sex, race/ethnicity, insurance status) and results reported?
signalling_questions.detail: AI models can perform well on average while being substantially worse for minority groups. Fairness analysis is now required by major journals.

required_for_low_overall_rob.description: These specific signalling questions, if answered No/Probably No, will result in High ROB regardless of other answers.
